items.title: Module 1 - Utilitarianism
items.plainText: Utilitarianism is the ethical theory that the right action is the one that produces the greatest good for the greatest number. Developed by Jeremy Bentham and refined by John Stuart Mill, utilitarianism evaluates actions by consequences rather than by fixed duties. A strong utilitarian answer compares likely gains and harms across everyone affected instead of focusing on one person's feelings alone. Utilitarianism is not just 'do whatever feels helpful.' It asks for a disciplined comparison of total consequences across the whole group. Use utilitarianism when an assignment asks you to compare likely outcomes, harms, benefits, and total well-being. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation." on p. 44. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters: pain and pleasure." on p. 43. John Stuart Mill wrote, "Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse." on p. 49. John Stuart Mill wrote, "It is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied." on p. 51.

items.html:
Module 1 - Utilitarianism

Core idea

Utilitarianism is the ethical theory that the right action is the one that produces the greatest good for the greatest number.

Why it matters

Developed by Jeremy Bentham and refined by John Stuart Mill, utilitarianism evaluates actions by consequences rather than by fixed duties. A strong utilitarian answer compares likely gains and harms across everyone affected instead of focusing on one person's feelings alone.

Common confusion

Utilitarianism is not just 'do whatever feels helpful.' It asks for a disciplined comparison of total consequences across the whole group.

Assignment move

Use utilitarianism when an assignment asks you to compare likely outcomes, harms, benefits, and total well-being.

items.title: Module 2 - Deontology
items.plainText: Deontology holds that actions are morally right or wrong based on duties and rules, regardless of whether breaking them would produce a better outcome. Immanuel Kant argues that moral law comes from reason and must apply universally. A deontological answer can reject a useful result when getting it requires lying, coercion, or using another person unfairly. Deontology is not about being rigid for its own sake. It protects the idea that some actions stay wrong even when they would be convenient. Use deontology when you need to show why a moral rule should constrain action even under pressure. Immanuel Kant wrote, "Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law." on p. 62. Immanuel Kant wrote, "Treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end." on p. 64.

items.title: Module 3 - Virtue Ethics
items.plainText: Virtue ethics focuses on the character of the moral agent rather than on rules or consequences alone. Aristotle argues that virtues are stable habits developed through practice. Instead of asking only 'What rule applies?' or 'What outcome is best?', virtue ethics asks what courage, honesty, justice, and practical wisdom require here. Virtue ethics is not vague niceness. It is a disciplined way of naming the trait that fits the situation and the habits the action is building. Use virtue ethics when a prompt asks what kind of person a decision cultivates over time. Aristotle wrote, "Virtue is a state of character concerned with choice, lying in a mean relative to us." on p. 80. Aristotle wrote, "We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit." on p. 78.

items.title: Module 2 - Categorical Imperative
items.plainText: Kant's categorical imperative is the supreme moral principle: act only on a maxim you could will to become a universal law. Its Formula of Universal Law tests whether your principle could apply to everyone without contradiction. Its Formula of Humanity requires treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as tools for someone else's plan. The categorical imperative is not just 'follow rules.' It asks whether the rule itself could be universal and whether people are being reduced to tools. Use the categorical imperative when you need to test a maxim or ask whether a choice treats people as ends rather than means. Immanuel Kant wrote, "Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law." on p. 62. Immanuel Kant wrote, "Treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end." on p. 64.

items.title: Module 1 - Felicific Calculus
items.plainText: Bentham's felicific calculus is a method for estimating how much pleasure and pain an action is likely to produce. The calculus weighs intensity, duration, certainty, propinquity, fecundity, purity, and extent. It turns utilitarian reasoning into a structured comparison rather than a vague guess about what seems nice. The felicific calculus is not the whole of utilitarianism. It is Bentham's method for making consequence comparison systematic. Use felicific calculus when you need to explain how Bentham would compare competing outcomes in detail. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation." on p. 44. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters: pain and pleasure." on p. 43.

items.title: Module 3 - Doctrine of the Mean
items.plainText: Aristotle's doctrine of the mean states that virtue is a middle ground between excess and deficiency. Courage lies between cowardice and recklessness. Generosity lies between stinginess and extravagance. The mean is not mathematical; it is the fitting balance relative to the person and the situation. The mean is not mediocrity. It is the right response between two moral failures. Use the doctrine of the mean when you need to name the balanced virtue between two bad extremes. Aristotle wrote, "Virtue is a state of character concerned with choice, lying in a mean relative to us." on p. 80. Aristotle wrote, "We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit." on p. 78.

items.title: Module 4 - Experience Machine
items.plainText: Robert Nozick's experience machine asks whether you would plug into a simulation that guarantees a perfectly pleasant life. If pleasure were the only thing that mattered, plugging in should be an easy choice. Most people refuse, suggesting that reality, achievement, and authentic relationships matter in ways a simple pleasure score cannot capture. The experience machine is not a utilitarian method. It is an objection meant to test whether pleasure alone can define the good life. Use the experience machine when you need to challenge a purely pleasure-based account of value. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation." on p. 44. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters: pain and pleasure." on p. 43. John Stuart Mill wrote, "Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse." on p. 49. John Stuart Mill wrote, "It is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied." on p. 51. Robert Nozick wrote, "The experience machine shows that something matters to us in addition to experience: we want to actually do things, not just have the experience of doing them." on p. 92.

items.title: Module 4 - Act vs Rule Utilitarianism
items.plainText: Act utilitarianism judges each action directly by consequences, while rule utilitarianism asks which general rules would produce the best overall results if widely followed. Act utilitarianism is flexible but can justify unfairness in individual cases. Rule utilitarianism trades some flexibility for stability, arguing that rules protecting trust and rights often produce better long-term outcomes. These are not two different moral worlds. They are two ways of applying utilitarian reasoning with different tradeoffs between flexibility and stability. Use act versus rule utilitarianism when a case asks whether you should judge a single act or the rule that would govern many acts. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation." on p. 44. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters: pain and pleasure." on p. 43. John Stuart Mill wrote, "Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse." on p. 49. John Stuart Mill wrote, "It is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied." on p. 51.

items.title: Module 5 - Social Contract Theory
items.plainText: Social contract theory holds that moral and political rules are justified because rational people would agree to them under fair conditions. Hobbes stresses escape from the chaos of the state of nature, while Rawls asks what principles people would choose behind a veil of ignorance. The focus is fair cooperation, reciprocity, and rules no one could reasonably reject from the start. Social contract theory is not just obedience to power. Its question is what rules free and equal people could fairly agree to. Use social contract theory when a case turns on fair rules, consent, and designing institutions under uncertainty. John Rawls wrote, "Justice is the first virtue of social institutions, as truth is of systems of thought." on p. 102. John Rawls wrote, "No one knows his place in society, his class position or social status. This ensures that the principles of justice are chosen behind a veil of ignorance." on p. 104.

items.title: Module 5 - Moral Relativism
items.plainText: Moral relativism holds that moral judgments are relative to cultural, societal, or individual standards rather than universally true for everyone. Cultural relativism helps explain disagreement across societies, but critics argue that pure relativism makes it hard to condemn practices like slavery or genocide in any principled way. The challenge is separating tolerance from moral paralysis. Relativism is not the same as being open-minded. It is the stronger claim that moral truth itself changes with the standpoint or culture. Use moral relativism when a prompt asks how to understand moral disagreement and whether universal criticism is still possible.

items.title: Module 6 - Trolley Problem
items.plainText: The trolley problem asks whether it is permissible to sacrifice one person in order to save five others. The lever version and the footbridge version produce the same arithmetic but very different moral intuitions. That gap exposes a live tension between consequentialist reasoning, duty-based constraints, and character-sensitive judgment. The trolley problem is not just a puzzle about arithmetic. It is a pressure test for what different ethical frameworks actually prioritize. Use the trolley problem when you need one case that makes utilitarian, deontological, and virtue-ethical intuitions visibly diverge.

items.title: Module 6 - Natural Law Theory
items.plainText: Natural law theory holds that moral standards are grounded in human nature and can be discovered through reason. Thomas Aquinas argues that moral order is not arbitrary; it fits the goods built into human flourishing. Modern versions often translate that idea into secular language about what supports or frustrates human well-being. Natural law is not just 'what feels natural.' It is the claim that reason can discover real moral structure in human flourishing. Use natural law theory when you need to explain how objective moral standards could be rooted in human nature itself. Thomas Aquinas wrote, "The natural law is nothing else than the rational creature's participation in the eternal law." on p. 118. Thomas Aquinas wrote, "Good is to be done and pursued, and evil is to be avoided." on p. 120.

items.title: AEONTHRA Demo Textbook
items.plainText: Ethics asks how one should live, what counts as a good life, and how reasons should guide action. Moral reasoning often weighs duties, consequences, and character at the same time, even when one framework seems dominant. When students read philosophy quickly, they need a way to connect definitions, objections, examples, and live tensions without losing the thread of the argument. A useful study system highlights core concepts, turns them into teach-back prompts, traces how each concept supports assignments, and exposes the elegant mistakes that sound right at first. Learning becomes more durable when discovery, retrieval, conflict, redesign, and reflection happen together instead of in separate disconnected tools. A premium learning protocol should help the student feel the idea before naming it, rebuild it from multiple angles, interrupt wrong versions, perform structural surgery, and prepare future recovery.

Immanuel Kant wrote, "Treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end." on p. 64.

items.html:
Module 2 - Categorical Imperative

Core idea

Kant's categorical imperative is the supreme moral principle: act only on a maxim you could will to become a universal law.

Why it matters

Its Formula of Universal Law tests whether your principle could apply to everyone without contradiction. Its Formula of Humanity requires treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as tools for someone else's plan.

Common confusion

The categorical imperative is not just 'follow rules.' It asks whether the rule itself could be universal and whether people are being reduced to tools.

Assignment move

Use the categorical imperative when you need to test a maxim or ask whether a choice treats people as ends rather than means.

items.title: Module 1 - Felicific Calculus
items.plainText: Bentham's felicific calculus is a method for estimating how much pleasure and pain an action is likely to produce. The calculus weighs intensity, duration, certainty, propinquity, fecundity, purity, and extent. It turns utilitarian reasoning into a structured comparison rather than a vague guess about what seems nice. The felicific calculus is not the whole of utilitarianism. It is Bentham's method for making consequence comparison systematic. Use felicific calculus when you need to explain how Bentham would compare competing outcomes in detail. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation." on p. 44. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters: pain and pleasure." on p. 43.

items.title: Module 3 - Doctrine of the Mean
items.plainText: Aristotle's doctrine of the mean states that virtue is a middle ground between excess and deficiency. Courage lies between cowardice and recklessness. Generosity lies between stinginess and extravagance. The mean is not mathematical; it is the fitting balance relative to the person and the situation. The mean is not mediocrity. It is the right response between two moral failures. Use the doctrine of the mean when you need to name the balanced virtue between two bad extremes. Aristotle wrote, "Virtue is a state of character concerned with choice, lying in a mean relative to us." on p. 80. Aristotle wrote, "We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit." on p. 78.

items.title: Module 4 - Experience Machine
items.plainText: Robert Nozick's experience machine asks whether you would plug into a simulation that guarantees a perfectly pleasant life. If pleasure were the only thing that mattered, plugging in should be an easy choice. Most people refuse, suggesting that reality, achievement, and authentic relationships matter in ways a simple pleasure score cannot capture. The experience machine is not a utilitarian method. It is an objection meant to test whether pleasure alone can define the good life. Use the experience machine when you need to challenge a purely pleasure-based account of value. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation." on p. 44. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters: pain and pleasure." on p. 43. John Stuart Mill wrote, "Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse." on p. 49. John Stuart Mill wrote, "It is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied." on p. 51. Robert Nozick wrote, "The experience machine shows that something matters to us in addition to experience: we want to actually do things, not just have the experience of doing them." on p. 92.

items.title: Module 4 - Act vs Rule Utilitarianism
items.plainText: Act utilitarianism judges each action directly by consequences, while rule utilitarianism asks which general rules would produce the best overall results if widely followed. Act utilitarianism is flexible but can justify unfairness in individual cases. Rule utilitarianism trades some flexibility for stability, arguing that rules protecting trust and rights often produce better long-term outcomes. These are not two different moral worlds. They are two ways of applying utilitarian reasoning with different tradeoffs between flexibility and stability. Use act versus rule utilitarianism when a case asks whether you should judge a single act or the rule that would govern many acts. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation." on p. 44. Jeremy Bentham wrote, "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters: pain and pleasure." on p. 43. John Stuart Mill wrote, "Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse." on p. 49. John Stuart Mill wrote, "It is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied." on p. 51.

items.title: Module 5 - Social Contract Theory
items.plainText: Social contract theory holds that moral and political rules are justified because rational people would agree to them under fair conditions. Hobbes stresses escape from the chaos of the state of nature, while Rawls asks what principles people would choose behind a veil of ignorance. The focus is fair cooperation, reciprocity, and rules no one could reasonably reject from the start. Social contract theory is not just obedience to power. Its question is what rules free and equal people could fairly agree to. Use social contract theory when a case turns on fair rules, consent, and designing institutions under uncertainty. John Rawls wrote, "Justice is the first virtue of social institutions, as truth is of systems of thought." on p. 102. John Rawls wrote, "No one knows his place in society, his class position or social status. This ensures that the principles of justice are chosen behind a veil of ignorance." on p. 104.

items.title: Module 5 - Moral Relativism
items.plainText: Moral relativism holds that moral judgments are relative to cultural, societal, or individual standards rather than universally true for everyone. Cultural relativism helps explain disagreement across societies, but critics argue that pure relativism makes it hard to condemn practices like slavery or genocide in any principled way. The challenge is separating tolerance from moral paralysis. Relativism is not the same as being open-minded. It is the stronger claim that moral truth itself changes with the standpoint or culture. Use moral relativism when a prompt asks how to understand moral disagreement and whether universal criticism is still possible.

items.title: Module 6 - Trolley Problem
items.plainText: The trolley problem asks whether it is permissible to sacrifice one person in order to save five others. The lever version and the footbridge version produce the same arithmetic but very different moral intuitions. That gap exposes a live tension between consequentialist reasoning, duty-based constraints, and character-sensitive judgment. The trolley problem is not just a puzzle about arithmetic. It is a pressure test for what different ethical frameworks actually prioritize. Use the trolley problem when you need one case that makes utilitarian, deontological, and virtue-ethical intuitions visibly diverge.

items.title: Module 6 - Natural Law Theory
items.plainText: Natural law theory holds that moral standards are grounded in human nature and can be discovered through reason. Thomas Aquinas argues that moral order is not arbitrary; it fits the goods built into human flourishing. Modern versions often translate that idea into secular language about what supports or frustrates human well-being. Natural law is not just 'what feels natural.' It is the claim that reason can discover real moral structure in human flourishing. Use natural law theory when you need to explain how objective moral standards could be rooted in human nature itself. Thomas Aquinas wrote, "The natural law is nothing else than the rational creature's participation in the eternal law." on p. 118. Thomas Aquinas wrote, "Good is to be done and pursued, and evil is to be avoided." on p. 120.

items.title: AEONTHRA Demo Textbook
items.plainText: Ethics asks how one should live, what counts as a good life, and how reasons should guide action. Moral reasoning often weighs duties, consequences, and character at the same time, even when one framework seems dominant. When students read philosophy quickly, they need a way to connect definitions, objections, examples, and live tensions without losing the thread of the argument. A useful study system highlights core concepts, turns them into teach-back prompts, traces how each concept supports assignments, and exposes the elegant mistakes that sound right at first. Learning becomes more durable when discovery, retrieval, conflict, redesign, and reflection happen together instead of in separate disconnected tools. A premium learning protocol should help the student feel the idea before naming it, rebuild it from multiple angles, interrupt wrong versions, perform structural surgery, and prepare future recovery.
